en.extract:
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker (Dicaeum trochileum) is a bird species in the family of Dicaeidae. It is a species endemic to Indonesia. This flowerpecker inhabits a few islands of the archipelago of Indonesia. It is mainly observed in open wooden areas, gardens, and mangroves.
As of today, D. trochileum population is stable. The IUCN has classified its conservation status to Least Concern.
This species is not well studied.


Description
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker body length can reach up to eight to nine centimeters after maturity.
The red to orange upper body of the males is the main characteristic that distinguishes them from the females. Their red plumage covers their head, their back, their rump and their throat. Their wings are black with blue pigmentation. Their legs are black, and their beak is grey. The tip of their tail is black.
The females have a light grey to brown head and back. Their orange to red rumps characterize the females' morphology. Similarly to the males, their legs are black, their beak is grey, and the tip of their tail is black. Their wings are lighter than the male's wings colour, and they are missing the blue coloration.
The morphology of the juveniles resembles more that of the females than that of the males. The small size and lighter body colours characterize the youngs. They have a light grey beak with pale brown wings and a pale grey to yellow belly.


Taxonomy
This species is from the family Dicaeidae and in the genus Dicaeum. This genus was first named by George Cuvier, a French anatomist, in 1817. The species' name Dicaeum trochileum was given by Anders Sparrman in 1789.


Distribution and habitat
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker is endemic to a few islands of the archipelago of Indonesia. It mainly inhabits the islands of Java and Bali. A few individuals have been reported on Lombok and Sumatra islands, and in Southeastern Borneo.
It mostly inhabits open woodlands, gardens and mangroves. This bird's presence has also been reported on the seacoast and in the lowlands of Sumatra Island. It is found between the sea level and up to a maximum of 600 meters in altitude.
Many tourists travel to the region of Taro on the island of Bali to observe this bird.


Behavior


Vocalization
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker can be easily recognized by its call. It makes high and acute short notes. Its vocals are either a "zit-zit-zit" sound or "seeeeep…seeeeep". Every pitch last from two to three and a half seconds.


Diet
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker feeds mostly on small berries, insects, nectar and pollen. It is often seen in milkweed and mistletoe grooves, foraging the plants' berries. The aggressivity of this small bird allows it to perch low in trees and its small size helps it to move through the dense branches of the mistletoes to find its berries. It will forage either alone or with a partner.


Reproduction
The nest of this species is about 70–80 × 30–35 mm made from grass and leaves. Milkweed fibers have also been found in their nests. Their eggs are white, sometimes glossed and they have a dark red dot at one end. This flowerpecker lays the eggs in a large hole dug in the middle of the nests.
Both males and females are found in the nest, taking care of their chick together.


Moulting and breeding
There are individuals of scarlet-headed flowerpecker moulting all year round, but a higher number of individuals moult in July. The individuals calibrate their moulting with the abundance and quality of the food resources they can find.
The breeding season of this species changes depending on the island and the region each individual inhabits. In West Java, the breeding season extends from January to October whereas, in East and Central Java, it is generally from April to May.


History
In 1938, the morphology of some unidentified bird species exhibited in the Buitenzorg Museum in Borneo was analyzed because of the similarities between their morphology, and that of the scarlet-headed flowerpecker and the sundanese flowerpecker (Dicaeum cruentatum). The unidentified bird species have been hypothesized to be a hybridization of these two flowerpeckers. The bird found have similarly colored underparts, wings and tails feathers as in both flowerpeckers. Its other morphological features are either similar to the scarlet-headed flowerpecker or to the Sundanese flowerpecker. An overlap of their habitat could explain how the two birds could have reproduced together.


Conservation status
This species's conservation status is Least concern. The human presence and the noise disturbance affect this bird's activity during the day. This flowerpecker is more active in the morning when it is silent and deserted of human presence. However, those disturbances do not affect the population's reproduction and survival, making this species' population's number stable.


References


External links
Scarlet-headed flowerpecker vocalization: https://eol.org/pages/1051724/media?subclass=sound
Birds of the World: https://birdsoftheworld-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/bow/species/schflo1/cur/introduction#mig

The '''scarlet-headed flowerpecker''' ('''''Dicaeum trochileum''''') is a bird species in the family of ''[[Flowerpecker|Dicaeidae]]''. It is a species [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Indonesia]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Taufiqurrahman|first=Imam|date=2010|title=Observation of presumed nest-building by Scarlet-headed Flowerpecker Dicaeum trochileum in Yogyakarta, Java, Indonesia|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/347278922|journal=Birding Asia|volume=14|pages=90–91}}</ref> This flowerpecker inhabits a few islands of the [[archipelago]] of [[Indonesia]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Robert Cheke & Clive Mann|date=2020|title=Scarlet-headed Flowerpecker|journal=Birds of the World|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/schflo1/cur/introduction|access-date=November 12, 2021}}</ref> It is mainly observed in open wooden areas, gardens, and mangroves.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last=Bishop|first=K. David|date=1985|title=Scarlet-headed Flowerpecker Dicaeum trochileum a new bird for Sumatra|url=http://kukila.org/index.php/KKL/article/view/7|journal=KUKILA|language=en|volume=2|issue=1|pages=7–8|issn=0216-9223}}</ref>

As of today'', D. trochileum'' population is stable. The IUCN has classified its conservation status to [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]].

This species is not well studied.

== Description ==
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker body length can reach up to eight to nine centimeters after maturity.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Syaqina|first1=T Y|last2=Mulyani|first2=Y A|last3=Hermawan|first3=R|date=2018-10-17|title=Birds activities at urban greenways in Bogor|journal=IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environmental Science|volume=179|issue=1|article-number=012042|doi=10.1088/1755-1315/179/1/012042|bibcode=2018E&ES..179a2042S|s2cid=186371583|issn=1755-1315|doi-access=free}}</ref>

The red to orange upper body of the males is the main characteristic that distinguishes them from the females.<ref name=":1" /> Their red plumage covers their head, their back, their rump and their throat.<ref name=":1" /> Their wings are black with blue pigmentation.<ref name=":1" /> Their legs are black, and their beak is grey.<ref name=":1" /> The tip of their tail is black.<ref name=":1" />

The females have a light grey to brown head and back.<ref name=":1" /> Their orange to red rumps characterize the females' morphology.<ref name=":1" /> Similarly to the males, their legs are black, their beak is grey, and the tip of their tail is black.<ref name=":1" /> Their wings are lighter than the male's wings colour, and they are missing the blue coloration.<ref name=":1" />

The morphology of the juveniles resembles more that of the females than that of the males. The small size and lighter body colours characterize the youngs. They have a light grey beak with pale brown wings and a pale grey to yellow belly.<ref name=":1" />

== Taxonomy ==
This species is from the family ''[[Flowerpecker|Dicaeidae]]'' and in the genus ''[[Dicaeum]].'' This genus was first named by George Cuvier, a French anatomist, in 1817.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Cuvier|first1=Georges|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/18030|title=Le règne animal distribué d'après son organisation: pour servir de base a l'histoire naturelle des animaux et d'introduction a l'anatomie comparée|last2=Laurillard|first2=Charles Léopold|last3=Pierron|first3=Jean Antoine|last4=Louvet|first4=G. P.|last5=Latreille|first5=P. A.|date=1817|publisher=Chez Déterville|volume=1|location=A Paris}}</ref> The species' name ''[[Dicaeum trochileum]]'' was given by [[Anders Sparrman]] in 1789.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Salomonsen|first=Finn|date=1961|title=Notes on Flowerpeckers (Aves, Dicaeidae) Dicaeum igniferum and its Derivatives|url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstream/handle/2246/3436/N2057.pdf?sequence=1|journal=American Museum Novitates}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker is [[Endemism|endemic]] to a few islands of the [[archipelago]] of [[Indonesia]]. It mainly inhabits the islands of [[Java]] and [[Bali]].<ref name=":0" /> A few individuals have been reported on [[Lombok]] and [[Sumatra]] islands, and in Southeastern [[Borneo]].<ref name=":0" />

It mostly inhabits open woodlands, gardens and mangroves.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> This bird's presence has also been reported on the seacoast and in the lowlands of Sumatra Island.<ref name=":0" /> It is found between the sea level and up to a maximum of 600 meters in altitude.<ref name=":1" />

Many tourists travel to the region of Taro on the island of [[Bali]] to observe this bird.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Ginantra, Darmadi, Gari, Sudirga|first=I Ketut, A.A. Ketut, Ni Made, Sang Ketut|date=2020|title=Bird Diversity as a Tourist Attraction in the Tourism Area of the Taro Gianyar Bali|url=https://simdos.unud.ac.id/uploads/file_penelitian_1_dir/efbab52c212d9e06fa7471bb1172dc12.pdf|journal=Journal of Biological and Chemical Research|volume=37|pages=28–35}}</ref>

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalization ===
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker can be easily recognized by its call. It makes high and acute short notes.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web|title=Scarlet-headed Flowerpecker - eBird|url=https://ebird.org/species/schflo1|access-date=2021-11-12|website=ebird.org|language=en}}</ref> Its vocals are either a "zit-zit-zit" sound or "seeeeep…seeeeep".<ref name=":1" /> Every pitch last from two to three and a half seconds.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":1" />

=== Diet ===
The scarlet-headed flowerpecker feeds mostly on small berries, insects, nectar and pollen.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is often seen in milkweed and mistletoe grooves, foraging the plants' berries.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Sedayu|first1=AGUNG|last2=Sumadijaya|first2=ALEX|date=2012-12-01|title=Host Specificity and Characteristics of Viscum ovalifolium in Pulau Dua Mangrove, Banten, Indonesia|journal=HAYATI Journal of Biosciences|language=en|volume=19|issue=4|pages=177–182|doi=10.4308/hjb.19.4.177|issn=1978-3019|doi-access=free}}</ref> The aggressivity of this small bird allows it to perch low in trees and its small size helps it to move through the dense branches of the mistletoes to find its berries.<ref name=":5" /> It will forage either alone or with a partner.<ref name=":1" />

=== Reproduction ===
The nest of this species is about 70–80 × 30–35 mm made from grass and leaves.<ref name=":0" /> Milkweed fibers have also been found in their nests.<ref name=":5" /> Their eggs are white, sometimes glossed and they have a dark red dot at one end.<ref name=":1" /> This flowerpecker lays the eggs in a large hole dug in the middle of the nests.<ref name=":1" />

Both males and females are found in the nest, taking care of their chick together.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=TAUFIQURRAHMAN|first=Imam|date=2011|title=Nesting record of Blood-breasted Flowerpecker Dicaeum sanguinolentum in Gunung Merapi National Park, Yogyakarta, Indonesia|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/295654644|journal=Forktail}}</ref>

=== Moulting and breeding ===
There are individuals of scarlet-headed flowerpecker [[moulting]] all year round, but a higher number of individuals moult in July.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Mulyani|first1=Yeni Aryati|last2=Tirtaningtyas|first2=Fransisca Noni|last3=Hadi|first3=Nanang Khairul|last4=Dewi|first4=Lina Kristina|last5=Kaban|first5=Aronika|date=2017-10-01|title=Molt in Birds Inhabiting a Human-Dominated Habitat|journal=HAYATI Journal of Biosciences|language=en|volume=24|issue=4|pages=195–200|doi=10.1016/j.hjb.2017.11.004|issn=1978-3019|doi-access=free}}</ref> The individuals calibrate their moulting with the abundance and quality of the food resources they can find.<ref name=":6" />

The breeding season of this species changes depending on the island and the region each individual inhabits.<ref name=":0" /> In West Java, the breeding season extends from January to October whereas, in East and Central Java, it is generally from April to May.<ref name=":0" />

== History ==
In 1938, the [[Morphology (linguistics)|morphology]] of some unidentified bird species exhibited in the Buitenzorg Museum in Borneo was analyzed because of the similarities between their morphology, and that of the scarlet-headed flowerpecker and the sundanese flowerpecker (''[[Scarlet-backed flowerpecker|Dicaeum cruentatum]])''.<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=VOOUS, K.H. & VANBEMMEL, A.C.V|date=1949|title=ON A CASE OF HYBRIDIZATION IN DICAEUM (Aves, Dicaeidae)|url=https://e-journal.biologi.lipi.go.id/index.php/treubia/article/viewFile/2619/2256|journal=Treubia|volume=20|pages=35–37}}</ref> The unidentified bird species have been hypothesized to be a hybridization of these two flowerpeckers.<ref name=":7" /> The bird found have similarly colored underparts, wings and tails feathers as in both flowerpeckers. Its other morphological features are either similar to the scarlet-headed flowerpecker or to the Sundanese flowerpecker.<ref name=":7" /> An overlap of their habitat could explain how the two birds could have reproduced together.<ref name=":7" />

== Conservation status ==
This species's conservation status is [[Least-concern species|Least concern]]. The human presence and the noise disturbance affect this bird's activity during the day.<ref name=":3" /> This flowerpecker is more active in the morning when it is silent and deserted of human presence. However, those disturbances do not affect the population's reproduction and survival, making this species' population's number stable.<ref name=":3" />

zh.extract:
红头啄花鸟（学名：Dicaeum trochileum），是啄花鸟科啄花鸟属的一种，为印度尼西亚的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
红头啄花鸟的栖息地包括亚热带或热带的（低地）湿润疏灌丛、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、耕地、种植园、亚热带或热带严重退化的前森林和城市。


参考文献

Dicaeum trochileum. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
